section.title: The Woman on the Beast
section.sectionRationale: Chapter 17 pauses the sequence of judgment to unpack, through an angel-guided vision and explicit interpretation, exactly who Babylon the great is: an idolatrous, blood-guilty empire pictured as a prostitute riding a monstrous beast. It is bounded by the angel's invitation in 17:1 to see the prostitute's punishment and the identification of the woman as the great city in 17:18, forming a self-contained interpretive unit between the bowls and Babylon's fall.
section.thesis: Babylon the great, an alluring alliance of idolatrous empire, wealth, and false religion, rides on the very beast that will one day turn and devour her, while the Lamb, though warred against by the kings of the earth, triumphs as Lord of lords and King of kings.
section.frameNote: Five beats: the angel's invitation to see the prostitute's judgment; the vision of the woman enthroned on the scarlet beast; the angel's interpretation of the beast, its heads, and its kings; the ten horns' war against the Lamb and His certain triumph; and the meaning of the waters and the woman as the great city that rules the kings of the earth.

units.label: The invitation to see the prostitute's judgment

verses.text: Then one of the seven angels with the seven bowls came and said to me, “Come, I will show you the punishment of the great {{porne|prostitute}}, who sits on many waters.
The kings of the earth were immoral with her, and those who dwell on the earth were intoxicated with the wine of her immorality.”

units.label: The vision: the woman on the scarlet beast
units.note: dazzling, drunk with the blood of the saints

verses.text: And the angel carried me away in the Spirit into a wilderness, where I saw a woman sitting on a scarlet {{therion|beast}} that was covered with blasphemous names and had seven heads and ten horns.
The woman was dressed in purple and scarlet, and adorned with gold and precious stones and pearls. She held in her hand a golden cup full of abominations and the impurities of her sexual immorality.
And on her forehead a {{mysterion|mysterious}} name was written: BABYLON THE GREAT, THE MOTHER OF PROSTITUTES AND OF THE ABOMINATIONS OF THE EARTH.
I could see that the woman was drunk with the blood of the saints and witnesses for Jesus. And I was utterly amazed at the sight of her.

units.label: The angel explains the beast, its heads, and its kings
units.note: a mind with wisdom is required

verses.text: “Why are you so amazed?” said the angel. “I will tell you the {{mysterion|mystery}} of the woman and of the beast that carries her, which has the seven heads and ten horns.

The beast that you saw, it was, and now is no more, but is about to come up out of the {{abyssos|Abyss}} and go to its destruction. And those who dwell on the earth whose names were not written in the Book of Life from the foundation of the world will marvel when they see the beast that was, and is not, and yet will be.
This calls for a mind with wisdom. The seven heads are seven mountains on which the woman sits.
There are also seven kings. Five have fallen, one is, and the other has not yet come; but when he does come, he must remain for only a little while.
The beast that was, and now is not, is an eighth king, who belongs to the other seven and is going into destruction.

units.label: The ten horns' war and the Lamb's triumph
units.note: Lord of lords and King of kings

verses.text: The ten horns you saw are ten kings who have not yet received a kingdom, but will receive one hour of authority as kings, along with the beast.
These kings have one purpose: to yield their power and authority to the beast.

They will make war against the Lamb, and the Lamb will triumph over them, because He is {{kyrios|Lord}} of lords and King of kings; and He will be accompanied by His called and chosen and faithful ones.”

units.label: The waters, the beast's hatred, and the meaning of the woman
units.note: the great city that rules the kings of the earth

verses.text: Then the angel said to me, “The waters you saw, where the prostitute was seated, are peoples and multitudes and nations and tongues.
And the ten horns and the beast that you saw will hate the prostitute. They will leave her desolate and naked, and will eat her flesh and burn her with fire.
For God has put it into their hearts to carry out His purpose by uniting to give their kingdom to the beast, until the words of God are fulfilled.
And the woman you saw is the great city that rules over the kings of the earth.”

porne.gloss: A prostitute or harlot. The word pictures a woman who sells herself, deliberately paired throughout the chapter with porneia, sexual immorality, itself a stock Old Testament metaphor for idolatry and covenant unfaithfulness, Hosea, Ezekiel 16, Jeremiah 3. Babylon the great is not literal prostitution but an empire whose idolatrous, seductive power draws the nations away from the true God.

therion.gloss: A wild beast, a monster. The word Revelation uses throughout for the persecuting political power introduced in chapter 13. Here the beast carries the woman, showing that the world's idolatrous religious and economic system rides upon, and is ultimately used and discarded by, brute political power.

mysterion.gloss: A mystery, a hidden truth now disclosed. In the New Testament a mysterion is not something obscure forever but a secret God chooses to unveil; here the angel promises to explain the mystery of the woman and the beast, and does so plainly in the verses that follow, showing that apocalyptic symbols are meant to be understood, not left as riddles.

kyrios.gloss: Lord, master, owner. Used throughout the Greek Old Testament to render the divine name, the title here crowns Jesus as Lord of lords, sovereign over every earthly king who wars against Him; His triumph is not future speculation but the certain outcome already declared.

abyssos.gloss: The abyss, the bottomless pit. Revelation's name for the prison of demonic powers, from which the beast emerges and to which it returns for final destruction; its appearance here anchors the beast's power as derivative and temporary, in contrast to the everlasting kingdom of Christ.

basic.context: An angel takes John in a vision to see a woman, a prostitute called Babylon, riding on a monstrous, many-headed beast. She is dressed in splendor and drunk with the blood of God's people, and the angel explains that the beast and its heads and horns stand for a succession of kings and kingdoms that will make war on the Lamb, and lose. In the end the very beast that carries the woman turns on her, and the vision explains that she is the great city that rules over the kings of the earth.
basic.hermeneutics: The identity of Babylon here is one of the most debated pictures in Revelation, whether it refers mainly to the Rome of John's own day, some future world system, or the recurring pattern of idolatrous civilization in every age, allied with worldly power. The debate is worth naming honestly rather than settling too quickly, but the chapter's central claims are not in doubt: this seductive alliance of empire and idolatry will be judged, will be destroyed by its own ally, and the Lamb will triumph regardless of how any reader identifies the details.
basic.originalLanguages: Babylon is a porne, a prostitute, the Old Testament's regular picture for idolatry dressed up as allure. She rides a therion, a beast, the same word for the persecuting power of chapter 13. The angel promises to unveil the mysterion, the mystery, showing that the vision's symbols are meant to be understood, not left as an unsolvable riddle. And at the chapter's center, Jesus is named kyrios of lords, Lord of lords, guaranteeing His victory over every power that wars against Him.
basic.typology: Babylon's portrait as a bejeweled, seductive prostitute drunk on the blood of the saints draws directly on Old Testament pictures of idolatrous nations and unfaithful cities, now brought to their final form and final judgment.
basic.christ: In the middle of this dark vision stands one certain fact: the Lamb triumphs. Every king who wars against Him loses, because He is Lord of lords and King of kings, and His called, chosen, and faithful people share in that victory with Him.
basic.god: God's sovereignty extends even over evil's self-destruction: it is God who puts it into the hearts of the beast and the ten horns to turn on the prostitute and destroy her, showing that even the alliance of evil powers unknowingly serves God's determined purpose.
basic.crossReferences: Jeremiah 51 and Isaiah 47 picture Babylon as a queen and sorceress judged by God. Ezekiel 16 and Hosea use the same image for unfaithful Israel. And Revelation 19 shows the Lamb's promised triumph finally realized as He returns as King of kings and Lord of lords.
basic.oneStory: From Israel's prophets to Revelation's final vision, Scripture pictures idolatry as adultery and empire as prostitution, seductive, wealthy, and ultimately self-destructive. But running through every page is the promise that the Lamb, however fiercely warred against, triumphs, and all who are called, chosen, and faithful triumph with Him.

context.literary: Chapter 17 functions as an interpretive pause within Revelation's judgment sequence, an angel-guided vision followed by explicit angelic interpretation, a literary form drawn from apocalyptic books like Daniel and Zechariah. It sits between the seven bowls in chapter 16 and Babylon's fall in chapter 18, supplying the explanation the reader needs before that fall is narrated in full.
context.historical: Babylon's description, seven hills, kings, and vast wealth drawn from many peoples, would have evoked Rome for John's first-century readers, whose imperial cult demanded worship and whose economy was inseparable from idolatry and, in Christian eyes, moral corruption. The vision assured persecuted believers that Rome's apparent permanence was illusory and that the empire's own allies would one day turn on it.
context.canonical: The chapter draws together Old Testament pictures of Babylon, Nineveh, and Tyre as prostitutes and the four-kingdom visions of Daniel into one climactic figure, while its promise of the Lamb's triumph as Lord of lords and King of kings anticipates Revelation 19's return of Christ under that same title.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To unveil the true character behind the world's most seductive and powerful idolatrous system, showing it to be doomed, self-destructive, and no match for the Lamb, so that believers are neither seduced by its wealth nor terrified by its beastly power.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The vision describes the nature and destiny of Babylon and the beast; it is not a coded political roadmap for identifying current nations. Its abiding truths are that idolatrous power dressed in wealth and seduction will be judged, that such alliances of evil eventually devour themselves, and that Christ's kingship over all earthly kings is certain.

debates.question: Who or what is Babylon the great: first-century Rome, a future world-system, or symbolic idolatrous civilization in every age?

views.label: First-century Rome
views.case: The seven hills of 17:9, the kings and vast wealth, and the persecution of the saints match imperial Rome closely enough that many read Babylon as a direct, thinly veiled portrait of Rome, judged within history through the empire's later collapse.

views.label: A future, end-time world-system
views.case: Others read Babylon as a still-future global economic, political, and religious system, since Rome fell without the total, catastrophic judgment chapter 18 describes, so the vision's complete fulfillment is reserved for the end of history.

views.label: A recurring symbol of idolatrous civilization in every age
views.case: A third reading treats Babylon as a transhistorical symbol, any culture, empire, or economy that seduces God's people into idolatry and complicity with violence against the saints, fulfilled partially in Rome and in every worldly power since, until its final form at history's end.

debates.bearing: The three readings need not be mutually exclusive: Rome was a genuine, historical Babylon in John's day, later powers have echoed the same pattern, and Scripture may intend a final, ultimate Babylon still to come; all three agree that whatever wears Babylon's character will be judged.

debates.question: What do the seven mountains and seven kings represent?

views.label: Seven hills of Rome and a sequence of Roman emperors
views.case: Rome was famously built on seven hills, and many early readers understood the seven kings as a sequence of Roman emperors, five already fallen, one reigning as John wrote, and one yet to come, anchoring the vision in first-century imperial history.

views.label: A symbolic totality of world power across history
views.case: Because the specific list of five fallen kings fits no single agreed sequence of emperors without strain, other readers take seven as a number of completeness, symbolizing the full sweep of world empires across history, with the vision's outline sketching a pattern rather than naming particular rulers.

debates.bearing: However the details are counted, the passage's point is that worldly power, however impressive or numerous its kings, is temporary and going into destruction, while the Lamb's kingdom alone endures.

study.originalLanguages: Babylon appears as a porne, a prostitute, continuing the Old Testament's frequent metaphor for idolatry as adultery; her allure, purple, scarlet, gold, and pearls, cannot disguise that she is drunk on the blood of the saints. She rides a therion, the same beast introduced in chapter 13, whose power rises from the abyssos, the abyss, the prison of demonic forces, marking its authority as derivative, borrowed, and doomed. The angel promises to disclose the mysterion, the mystery, of the woman and the beast, and does so plainly, since apocalyptic symbol in Scripture is meant to be understood rather than left forever obscure. And at the vision's turning point, the Lamb is declared kyrios of lords, Lord of lords, and King of kings, guaranteeing that however many kings war against Him, His triumph, and that of His called, chosen, and faithful people, is certain.

typology.type: Idolatrous nations pictured as prostitutes, Isaiah 47, Ezekiel 16, Nahum 3
typology.fulfillment: Babylon the great in Revelation 17, the final and fullest form of the Old Testament's picture of seductive, idolatrous, blood-guilty empire.

typology.type: The four beasts of Daniel 7 rising from the sea
typology.fulfillment: The scarlet beast with seven heads and ten horns, gathering the imagery of Daniel's successive empires into one final, composite power.

typology.type: God turning evil powers against each other, Ezekiel 38, Judges 7
typology.fulfillment: The ten horns and the beast turning to destroy the very prostitute they carried, showing God's sovereignty even over evil's self-destruction.

study.christ: At the center of a vision dominated by a monstrous beast and a blood-drunk prostitute stands a single, unshaken certainty: the Lamb will triumph. Kings unite their power against Him, yet He is declared Lord of lords and King of kings, titles Scripture reserves for God alone, now openly given to Jesus. His victory is not contested or uncertain; it is simply stated as fact, and those who share it are described not as spectators but as His called, chosen, and faithful people, who triumph with Him because they belong to Him.
study.god: God's sovereignty in this chapter reaches even into the plans of His enemies: it is God who puts it into the hearts of the beast and its horns to turn on the prostitute who rode it, so that evil, without knowing it, executes God's own purpose and fulfills His words. Nothing, not even the self-destructive treachery of the wicked, falls outside His governance.

crossReferences.note: Babylon addressed as a queen who will be humbled to the dust, background for her fall pictured here and in chapter 18.
Unfaithful Jerusalem pictured as a prostitute adorned with the very jewelry and fine linen later applied to Babylon.
The fourth beast with ten horns, the source of Revelation's composite beast with seven heads and ten horns.
The risen Christ returns bearing the very title given Him here, King of kings and Lord of lords, His triumph now fully realized.

study.oneStory: Scripture again and again pictures unfaithfulness to God as prostitution, dazzling, wealthy, and ultimately self-destructive, whether in ancient Nineveh, unfaithful Jerusalem, or Revelation's Babylon the great. This chapter shows that alliance of idolatry and power collapsing under its own treachery, while the Lamb, warred against by every king of the earth, simply and certainly triumphs, Lord of lords and King of kings, together with all who are called, chosen, and faithful to Him.

sermon.title: Revelation (Part 20): Revelation 17-18
sermon.why: Carson's sermon covers Revelation 17-18 directly, addressing the vision of the great prostitute drawn from Old Testament prophecy and the contrast between the prostitute and the bride, matching the exact passage and theme of this study.

quiz.q: What does the angel say the woman represents in verse 18?

options.text: The great city that rules over the kings of the earth, an alliance of idolatrous empire and worldly power.
options.note: Revelation 17:18 gives this explicit interpretation.

options.text: A literal individual woman who will appear at the end of history.
options.note: The angel identifies her as a city, a symbol of a system, not a literal individual.

options.text: The bride of the Lamb in disguise.
options.note: She is the great prostitute, the opposite figure to the bride introduced later in Revelation.

options.text: A minor character with no larger significance in the vision.
options.note: She is the central figure of the whole vision, explicitly interpreted by the angel.

quiz.q: What happens to the prostitute at the hands of the beast and its ten horns?

options.text: They turn on her, leaving her desolate and naked, and eat her flesh and burn her with fire.
options.note: Revelation 17:16 describes this reversal.

options.text: They defend and protect her from all future judgment.
options.note: The opposite happens; her own allies destroy her.

options.text: They crown her as their permanent queen.
options.note: They hate her and destroy her rather than honoring her.

options.text: They ignore her and move on to make war on the Lamb alone.
options.note: The text specifically describes their violent turn against her.

quiz.q: Why does the Lamb triumph over the kings who war against Him?

options.text: Because He is Lord of lords and King of kings, accompanied by His called, chosen, and faithful people.
options.note: Revelation 17:14 gives this reason directly.

options.text: Because the kings run out of resources to continue the war.
options.note: The text gives His identity as Lord of lords as the reason, not attrition.

options.text: Because an angel intervenes to disarm them.
options.note: No such angelic disarming is described; the Lamb Himself triumphs.

options.text: The outcome of the war is left genuinely uncertain in the text.
options.note: The text states plainly that the Lamb will triumph over them.

quiz.q: What does the angel promise to explain to John in verse 7?

options.text: The mystery of the woman and the beast that carries her, with its seven heads and ten horns.
options.note: Revelation 17:7 states this explicitly.

options.text: The exact date of the beast's final destruction.
options.note: The angel explains identity and meaning, not a date.

options.text: The names of every future world leader.
options.note: The explanation concerns the symbolic meaning of the vision, not a list of names.

options.text: Nothing; the angel refuses to explain anything further.
options.note: The angel promises, and gives, a detailed explanation in the verses that follow.

quiz.q: According to the angel, what has God put into the hearts of the beast and the ten horns in verse 17?

options.text: To carry out His purpose by uniting to give their kingdom to the beast, until God's words are fulfilled.
options.note: Revelation 17:17 gives this exact statement of God's sovereignty over evil's plans.

options.text: To repent and worship the Lamb.
options.note: The verse describes their unwitting service to God's purpose in judgment, not their repentance.

options.text: To rebuild the woman's kingdom stronger than before.
options.note: They destroy, rather than rebuild, the woman's kingdom.

options.text: Nothing; their actions happen entirely outside God's purposes.
options.note: The verse states plainly that God put this purpose into their hearts.

questions: Babylon is described as dazzling and seductive, dressed in gold, purple, and jewels, yet drunk on the blood of the saints. Where in your life is something seductive dressed up in a way that hides its true cost?

The very powers that carried the prostitute turn and destroy her. Have you seen alliances built on shared sin eventually turn on each other? What does that teach you about trusting anything built apart from God?
The Lamb triumphs, not by superior force in a fair fight, but simply because He is Lord of lords. Where do you need to rest in the certainty of His victory rather than your own strength?
God's called, chosen, and faithful ones share in the Lamb's triumph. What would it look like this week to actively identify yourself with Him rather than with the world's seductions?

The angel insists on explaining the mystery plainly rather than leaving John confused. When Scripture's symbols feel strange or frightening, how does it help to remember that God intends for His people to understand, not just to be unsettled?

translationNotes.note: Prostitute translates porne, sometimes rendered harlot in older translations; both render the same Greek word, and the choice reflects register rather than a difference in meaning.

Some manuscripts and translations print the name as a formal title in capitals, MYSTERY, BABYLON THE GREAT, treating mystery as the first word of the inscription itself rather than a description of the name; the BSB's a mysterious name was written reflects one legitimate way to render the construction.

Was, and now is no more, but is about to come echoes and inverts the divine title who is, and who was, and who is to come from Revelation 1:4 and 1:8, a deliberate parody marking the beast as a false, blasphemous copy of God's eternity.

Seven mountains and the count of five fallen, one is, one not yet come are among Revelation's most debated numerical details; interpreters differ on whether to press them as a precise historical sequence or read them as symbolic of complete, worldly power.

meta.notes: BSB text supplied pre-verified this session. No em dashes were present in the source verses for this chapter and none have been introduced. Poetic line breaks, where present in the source, were flattened into flowing prose per this repo's convention.
